county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1886; late 19th-century formation; Acadian/Cajun heartland
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge) and Acadia Parish Clerk of Court (Crowley). Heart of Cajun country — Acadian/Cajun families settled here after 1755 deportation from Nova Scotia. Formed 1886 from St. Landry; check St. Landry for earlier records. Catholic church registers at St. Michael the Archangel (Crowley) and surrounding chapels are essential for Acadian genealogy. Rice farming heritage dominates. Acadia Parish Library has local history collections. Cross-reference with Nova Scotia and New Brunswick records for Acadian origins. CODOFIL (Council for the Development of French in Louisiana) has cultural resources.

source_profiles.source_name: Acadia Parish Conveyance Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court, Crowley
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Conveyance (deed) books for Acadia Parish from 1886 forward; Louisiana uses civil law system — property transfers recorded as notarial acts

source_profiles.source_name: Acadia Parish Succession Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court, Crowley
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Succession records (Louisiana's civil law equivalent of probate) for Acadia Parish; includes inventories, family meetings (conseil de famille), and partition records naming heirs

source_profiles.source_name: Acadia Parish Marriage Records — Clerk of Court / Catholic Church Registers
source_profiles.repository: Acadia Parish Clerk of Court / Louisiana State Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil marriage licenses and Catholic church marriage registers for Acadia Parish; church records often predate civil registration
source_profiles.survival_notes: Statewide vital records registration from 1914; earlier records at parish level and in church registers

county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Allen Parish Clerk of Court (Oberlin). Formed 1912 from Calcasieu — a relatively young parish. Pre-1912 records in Calcasieu. Coushatta Tribe of Louisiana has a reservation in Allen Parish at Elton — check tribal enrollment records and BIA records at NARA. Oberlin was a lumber town; check sawmill and timber company records. Allen Parish Library has limited local history materials. Cross-reference with Calcasieu and Beauregard for related families. Mixed Cajun, Anglo-American, and Native American heritage in this transitional zone between Cajun prairies and piney woods.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; River Road plantation region
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Ascension Parish Clerk of Court (Donaldsonville). One of the original Orleans Territory parishes (1807). Donaldsonville briefly served as state capital (1830-1831). Major sugar plantation parish with extensive enslaved persons records. Catholic church registers at Ascension of Our Lord Church (Donaldsonville) date to colonial era. Notarial records at the parish clerk's office are rich for successions and property transfers under Louisiana civil law. French and Spanish colonial records at Louisiana State Archives. Acadians settled along Bayou Lafourche in the 1760s. River Road plantation records are extensive.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; Acadian/Cajun heartland
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Assumption Parish Clerk of Court (Napoleonville). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). Sugar plantation country along Bayou Lafourche. Catholic church records at Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary (Napoleonville) date to the French colonial period. Extensive enslaved persons records in succession files and notarial acts. Madewood Plantation archives document sugar planter families. Acadian families settled along Bayou Lafourche. Cross-reference with Lafourche and Terrebonne for related families. The parish has significant French Creole heritage and cultural resources at the Assumption Parish Library.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; Central Louisiana; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Avoyelles Parish Clerk of Court (Marksville). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). Named for the Avoyel Native American tribe. Tunica-Biloxi Tribe of Louisiana is headquartered in Marksville — tribal records and the Tunica-Biloxi Cultural and Educational Resource Center are important. Marksville prehistoric Indian site. French and Spanish colonial records. Mixed French, Creole, and Anglo-American heritage. Catholic church registers at St. Paul the Apostle (Mansura) and Immaculate Conception (Marksville). Notarial archives contain colonial-era acts. Gens de couleur libres (free people of color) community records.

county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Beauregard Parish Clerk of Court (DeRidder). Formed 1912 from Calcasieu — a relatively young parish. Pre-1912 records in Calcasieu. Piney woods region with lumber industry heritage. DeRidder was a railroad and lumber boom town. Fort Polk (now JRTC) military records at NARA for 20th-century research. Beauregard Parish Library has local history. Anglo-American settlers from Mississippi, Alabama, and East Texas predominate. Cross-reference with Calcasieu and Vernon parish records for related families. Some Cajun settlement in the southern portions near the prairie region.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1848 courthouse fire destroyed early Claiborne parent records; additional losses in Civil War period
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1848; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Bienville Parish Clerk of Court (Arcadia). Formed 1848 from Claiborne. BURNED PARISH — early records lost in courthouse fire. Named for Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne de Bienville, founder of New Orleans. Pre-1848 records in Claiborne. North Louisiana upland South culture — cotton farming, Protestant churches. Church records from Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian congregations supplement lost civil records. Bienville Parish Library has local genealogical materials. Check Louisiana State Archives for surviving state copies of tax rolls and militia records. Gibsland area history (Bonnie and Clyde ambush site).

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1843; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Bossier Parish Clerk of Court (Benton). Formed 1843 from Claiborne. Named for Pierre Evariste John Baptiste Bossier, Louisiana planter and US Congressman. Cotton plantation parish with significant enslaved population before 1865. Bossier City developed with Barksdale Air Force Base (1933) — military records at NARA. Pre-1843 records in Claiborne. Bossier Parish Library has the Bossier Parish Historical Center with local genealogical materials. Red River area plantation records. Cross-reference with Caddo and Claiborne.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1838; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Caddo Parish Clerk of Court (Shreveport). Formed 1838 from Natchitoches. Named for the Caddo Native Americans who ceded their lands in 1835. Shreveport became a major Red River port and cotton market. Shreveport Municipal Archives has extensive local records. LSU Shreveport — Noel Memorial Library Archives has regional manuscript collections. Caddo was a major plantation parish. Pre-1838 records in Natchitoches. Civil War records — Shreveport was the last Confederate capital of Louisiana. Oil boom (1906+) brought new settlers. Cross-reference Texas records for families crossing the border.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1910 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books and succession records
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Calcasieu Parish Clerk of Court (Lake Charles). Formed 1840 from St. Landry. BURNED PARISH — 1910 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books and succession records. Check Louisiana State Archives for surviving state copies. Southwest Louisiana — transition zone between Cajun culture and East Texas. Lake Charles was a lumber and rice center. McNeese State University Frazar Memorial Library has regional history collections. Parent parish for Allen, Beauregard, and Jefferson Davis (all 1912). Catholic and Protestant church records both important here.
